food_subgroup: Crustaceans — cooked slipper lobster in black pepper sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for slipper lobster (Thenus orientalis) prepared in black pepper sauce. FOODON_03411433 (lobster food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LN (Lobster) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for slipper lobster or black pepper sauce preparation in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227261009 (Lobster) is the closest available concept; no specific slipper lobster or black pepper preparation concept exists.

serving.description: 1 portion (shared dish, approximately 300 g edible portion per person)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Black Pepper Slipper Lobster is approximately 300 g of edible meat and sauce per person, usually shared as part of a multi-dish Zi Char or seafood meal.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked lobster (Homarus species / spiny lobster composite) with black pepper sauce — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lobster (NDB 15147) and USDA entry for black pepper sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy; IMR Malaysia MyFCD (reference for Malaysian seafood preparation context)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or HPB entry exists for slipper lobster (Thenus orientalis) in black pepper sauce as a prepared dish. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked lobster (NDB 15147 / FDC ID 175180) as the closest approved analogue for the crustacean base, with upward adjustment to energy, fat, and sodium to account for butter, black pepper sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce used in Zi Char preparation. Protein is slightly reduced from raw lobster to reflect sauce dilution. These are best-estimate values for a restaurant-prepared dish; exact values will vary by restaurant recipe.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of seafood and protein-dominant dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked crustacean / shellfish category; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Black Pepper Slipper Lobster. Slipper lobster is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate crustacean. GI is estimated at 5 based on the general principle that high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood dishes have very low glycemic indices (typically <15), consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database classification of seafood and protein-dominant foods. The black pepper sauce adds a small amount of carbohydrate (oyster sauce, soy sauce) but does not materially raise the GI. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (5 × 12.6) / 100 = 0.63, rounded to 0.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from black pepper sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Black Pepper Slipper Lobster due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 620 mg per 100 g (approximately 1860 mg per 300 g serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced sauce or sharing a smaller portion is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Black Pepper Slipper Lobster has a very low glycemic index (estimated GI ~5) and low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g per 100 g), making it a suitable protein-rich choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as white rice or mantou buns; however, the sodium content from the sauce warrants attention in those with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from butter-based black pepper sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While slipper lobster itself provides lean protein and beneficial micronutrients including selenium and zinc, the black pepper sauce preparation introduces significant sodium (~620 mg/100 g) and moderate saturated fat (~2.4 g/100 g) from butter, which may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles in individuals with cardiovascular disease; portion control and reduced-sauce preparation are recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Pepper Slipper Lobster is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean Zi Char seafood dish featuring slipper lobster (Thenus orientalis), a flat-bodied crustacean also known as flathead lobster or Moreton Bay bug, cooked in a bold, aromatic black pepper sauce. The dish is prepared by stir-frying or wok-cooking the lobster pieces at high heat with freshly cracked black pepper, butter, garlic, onion, oyster sauce, and soy sauce, producing a rich, glossy, intensely peppery coating. The texture of slipper lobster meat is firm, slightly sweet, and succulent, similar to spiny lobster but with a more delicate flavour. The black pepper sauce imparts a sharp, warming heat that is characteristic of Malaysian-style seafood cooking. This dish is commonly served at Zi Char restaurants and seafood houses in Singapore and Malaysia, often as a shared centrepiece dish alongside steamed rice or mantou (fried buns). Nutritionally, slipper lobster is a high-protein, low-fat seafood source rich in selenium, zinc, vitamin B12, and phosphorus. The prepared dish has an estimated energy content of approximately 142 kcal per 100 g, with 17.5 g protein and 5.8 g fat per 100 g. The primary clinical concern is the high sodium content (~620 mg/100 g) contributed by the oyster sauce and soy sauce in the black pepper sauce, making this dish a moderate-to-high sodium food that requires portion awareness for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is very low (estimated ~5), making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, USDA FoodData Central) for slipper lobster prepared in black pepper sauce as a restaurant dish. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked lobster (FDC ID 175180) with sauce-component adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate based on the low-carbohydrate, high-protein profile of the dish. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base crustacean data is from an approved primary source and the sauce adjustments are methodologically defensible.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for cooked lobster and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. 2. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this preparation. 3. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from butter and lobster lipid profiles; trans fat is estimated at 0.1 g/100 g from butter content and is not confirmed zero. 4. Sodium value of 620 mg/100 g is an upward estimate from the base cooked lobster value (~380 mg/100 g per USDA) to account for oyster sauce and soy sauce in the black pepper sauce preparation; actual restaurant values may vary significantly. 5. Halal status is marked false as the dish may contain oyster sauce with uncertain halal certification and butter; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 6. GI is estimated at 5 based on the very low carbohydrate content of the dish and the general classification of high-protein seafood dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database framework; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in any approved source. 7. The food_id GD003220 is preserved from the input seed record.
